text: after going to school we learned to make own decisions
comments: This indicates girls’ growing independent agency, so it opposes the norm that women lack decision-making power.

text: our mothers participate in trainings and do share their experiences at home
comments: Women gaining and sharing training knowledge suggests access to information rather than exclusion.

text: They motivate us do same thing in future
comments: This is aspirational because girls are encouraged toward future participation and advancement.

text: we need to seek permission from parents while going out with friends
comments: This confirms norms restricting girls’ mobility because movement requires parental permission.

text: Yes, education is important for us
comments: This is a general positive aspiration toward education without a specific occupation.

text: most of the boys study till Grade 12 (high school)
comments: This describes boys’ education attainment and does not itself indicate a strong unmet aspiration gap.

text: girls study till Bachelor Level
comments: This suggests comparatively high female attainment, so coded as no clear gap.

text: both girls and boys should do Master Level and more
comments: This is a clear education aspiration for advanced study for both sexes.

text: most of the boys end education after +12 (high school) and girls study till Bachelor level
comments: This shows boys’ lower attainment relative to desired higher education, indicating a gap.

text: educated women can handle a house easily
comments: This links women’s education to domestic capability, reflecting a gendered role expectation while still supporting education.

text: education not only means literate but also gives confidence
comments: This values education as empowerment and self-development rather than a specific job.

text: now every parent treats their children equally
comments: This directly expresses support for equality between sons and daughters.

text: now equal for both of them
comments: This indicates observed signs of gender equality in schooling.

text: young boys and girls also got opportunity to participate in 1 PM training
comments: This opposes information restriction because both sexes access training.

text: our parents, young girls and boys had participated in this training
comments: This shows inclusive knowledge access in training activities.

text: we help our parents to do seedling plantation during paddy cultivation, weeding and during harvesting crops
comments: This suggests girls participate in key farm tasks, opposing a rigid labor division.

text: due to poor economic condition of the family
comments: Poverty is a major barrier causing girls to end education, indicating a wide aspiration-attainment gap.

text: get marry at young age
comments: Early marriage interrupts girls’ education and future aspirations.

text: lack of awareness among parents
comments: Parental lack of awareness prevents girls’ continued schooling.

text: inability to study and detain in the class
comments: Academic struggle is identified as a barrier to completion.

text: due to work load at home
comments: Domestic work burden keeps girls from continuing education.

text: parents not educated and marry off at young age
comments: Low parental education and early marriage combine to widen girls’ education gap.

text: poor economic condition of the family and marriage at young age are the important reasons among girls to end education
comments: This explicitly identifies the two strongest reasons for girls’ educational discontinuation.

text: get job in private company
comments: Clear aspiration for salaried employment.

text: to become a social worker
comments: Clear occupational aspiration.

text: do job in any field
comments: Aspiration for employment without specifying a sector.

text: do household work and agricultural activities
comments: This reflects women’s expected domestic and farm work roles.

text: get married
comments: Marriage is presented as a standard life outcome for young women, limiting further independent pursuits.

text: do skilled trainings like tailoring and beauty parlor
comments: Skill training points to occupational aspirations in gendered service sectors.

text: village women are getting opportunity to participate in trainings and excursions for agricultural development
comments: This shows women are gaining information and exposure through formal channels.

text: off season farming
comments: Mention of new agricultural practice indicates some knowledge access.

text: maize cultivation and buffalo raising are important practice for women in the community
comments: Shows women involved in important productive activities, which nuances men-as-better-farmers norm.

text: now calf are born through IBF fertilization
comments: New livestock knowledge/practice is recognized by women.

text: earn more profits from maize cultivation
comments: This is an agricultural livelihood aspiration toward increased returns from farming.

text: tractor and thresher are important for men because they can plough land with an ease
comments: This confirms male association with heavy mechanized farming.

text: women are getting more opportunities such as girls do further education, opportunities to join trainings and excursions, forming groups and associations in the village
comments: This directly opposes information/opportunity exclusion for women.

text: girls do further education
comments: This reflects educational aspiration and opportunity for girls.

text: girls will not be allowed to do
comments: This confirms a gendered restriction on what girls may do in farming/selling.

text: parents think that someday girls will get marry and go with husband
comments: Restriction is justified by girls’ future marriage and departure, confirming gendered labor norms.

text: they will allow to do if work along with parents in the farmland
comments: Girls’ participation is allowed only conditionally, so norm is nuanced not fully open.

text: both mother and father do work together
comments: This opposes a strict division of labor because spouses work jointly.

text: there is provision of discounts if land is bought under women’s ownership
comments: This nuances asset restrictions by showing incentives for land in women’s names.

text: both men and women work together whoever owns the land and property
comments: Opposes strict separation of plots and labor by gender.

text: both men and women work together whoever owns the land and property
comments: Same reasoning: household members jointly work, not separately by gender.

text: agriculture office, husband and family members
comments: Women consult both institutions and family, so information access exists but is mediated.

text: husband and family members
comments: Decision-making is shared through consultation, not fully independent.

text: Ram Bahadur might allow Laxmi to join a local group of women who grow vegetables
comments: This opposes mobility restriction because participation in a women’s group is allowed.

text: Ram Bahadur might not allow Laxmi to join a local group of women grow vegetables
comments: Majority response confirms restriction on women joining groups.

text: Ram Bahadur will not allow laxmi to sell her vegetables herself
comments: Direct restriction on women’s independent market participation.

text: No, Ram Bahadur will not ask for less help rather he wants her to finish all his works
comments: Confirms women’s work burden continues even when they add new activities.

text: people will appreciate Sita for hard working
comments: Positive valuation of woman’s public economic role suggests support for equality.

text: people those who understand would appreciate her but people who do not understand would oppose her
comments: Mixed social reaction indicates stigma exists but is contested, so nuanced stigma.

text: friends who understand Hari will appreciate him for supporting wife
comments: Supportive reaction to husband sharing work suggests acceptance of more equal roles.

text: friends who do not understand him would say “you should let your wife do the household work rather than going out”
comments: This reflects criticism for violating gender norms, but not universal condemnation, so nuanced stigma.

text: Hari would be happy seeing her success and motive for more success in future
comments: This strongly supports women’s success and partnership.

text: gender equality is good thing there will be no more discrimination between men and women
comments: Direct endorsement of gender equality.

text: equal rights for women and girls as well
comments: Explicit support for women’s and girls’ equal rights.

text: Durga and Shyam will decide jointly to keep some crops for self consumption and some crops for selling
comments: Joint decision-making opposes restrictive norm of women lacking agency.

text: Shyam would be more likely to sell vegetables because he thinks Durga should take responsibilities of household and children
comments: Confirms conventional gender division: man sells, woman stays with domestic duties.

text: Shyam and Durga will sell vegetables turn wise as per convenience
comments: Opposes fixed labor division; selling is shared flexibly.

text: Durga and Shyam will do new farming practices to make more profits
comments: Shows acceptance of women’s agricultural knowledge and innovation.

text: because Durga has received that property from own parents
comments: This supports women’s independent rights over inherited property, opposing asset restriction.

text: Durga can make more profits from land
comments: This frames women’s land use as productive livelihood aspiration in agriculture.

text: Durga can expand farmland and make more crop production
comments: Agricultural expansion for profit is an aspirational productive goal.

text: Shyam is a man, it would be very easy for him to buy motorbike
comments: This confirms gender advantage in control over inherited money/assets.

text: Shyam will convince Durga to buy Motorbike
comments: Indicates male authority and greater power in household financial decisions.

text: Shyam has got the property from his parents
comments: Men’s inherited assets are treated as more easily actionable.

text: yes, we walk comfortably alone to the market
comments: This opposes strong restrictions on women’s market mobility.

text: young girls also go to the market for selling vegetables
comments: Shows girls’ market participation is accepted in practice.

text: married young women can also go alone to the market for selling crops and vegetables
comments: Opposes mobility restriction after marriage.

text: it is easy for a woman to sell in the local market because we go to market by riding bicycle
comments: Indicates practical ease and mobility for women sellers.

text: market is not far from XX
comments: Proximity reduces barriers to women’s market participation.

text: we can go out to public spaces in the community once we need to ask parents. But they will allow
comments: Mobility is broadly allowed, though with parental permission, so overall opposing strong restriction.

text: we will be allowed to go but need to seek permission from the parents first
comments: Migration/work mobility is possible but conditional on parental approval.

text: easier for boys
comments: This confirms gendered differences in mobility and work migration.

text: people will not say anything and would take it easily
comments: This opposes stigma around return migration.

text: girls get married at age of 20 years or above and boys at the 25 years or above
comments: Different marriage ages by sex reflect unequal gender expectations.

text: parents makes decisions for marriage of daughters
comments: Confirms girls’ restricted agency in marriage choice.

text: if fell in love with someone, first seek permission from the parents and then marry
comments: Some choice exists, but only with parental approval, so nuanced decision-making.

text: parents decision is important for marriage because they are our elders and we should respect their decisions
comments: Marriage should follow elders’ decisions, confirming family authority.

text: if I like a man I will let him meet my parents first
comments: Indicates partial choice, but through parental approval.

text: Soon after marriage women give birth to first baby
comments: Suggests a normative expectation of early childbearing after marriage.

text: at the age 22 or above women typically do have their first child
comments: Supports somewhat later childbearing as appropriate.

text: at this age a woman become physically fit to have first baby
comments: Positive evaluation of later maternal age.

text: a new mother mentally prepared herself to take care of new born baby
comments: Supports later birth based on maternal preparedness.

text: 22 years or above is a right age for a new mother to give birth
comments: Direct statement of preferred later age for first birth.

text: at this age a woman become physically fit as well as can take care of new born baby
comments: Explains why later childbearing is preferred.

text: both husband and wife decide together
comments: Opposes norm of women lacking reproductive decision-making power.

text: now a days, family do not put pressure on women to have child
comments: Indicates reduced coercion over fertility decisions.

text: now a days, educated married couple do family planning for 5-6 years
comments: Shows deliberate fertility planning within couples, especially educated ones.

text: do higher education and get jobs
comments: Clear aspiration for girls’ education and employment.

text: get skilled trainings like tailoring and beauty parlor
comments: Skill training linked to livelihood aspirations.

text: girls should get trainings on cottage industries
comments: This supports entrepreneurial/business skills for girls.

text: we should get more freedom like boys
comments: Directly indicates girls still have less freedom/mobility than boys.

text: boys should work in Nepal rather than going abroad for foreign employments
comments: Broad aspiration for local employment rather than migration.

text: boys should study higher like girls
comments: Aspiration for boys’ higher education, modeled on girls’ attainment.
